sources.notes: Statement and constraints: split an A/B string into single-letter and alternating length-2 blocks under four quotas.
Official editorial solution decomposes maximal alternating segments by endpoint orientation and greedily consumes the shortest useful segments for AB and BA quotas.

reference_candidates.notes: Luogu candidate source; not used in this review because the official editorial was sufficient.

annotation.summary: Decide whether a binary A/B string can be partitioned into A, B, AB, and BA blocks without exceeding the corresponding quotas.
annotation.constraints: Total string length is at most 5e5, so the solution must scan and sort segment lengths, not use DP over quotas.
annotation.core_idea: Only maximal alternating segments can contribute length-2 blocks. Odd alternating segments have equal endpoints and can be used flexibly as pairs after leaving one single. Even segments starting with A naturally provide AB pairs, and even segments starting with B naturally provide BA pairs. Use the shortest same-orientation segments first for their matching quota; any leftover even segment can still provide opposite-orientation pairs, but loses one pair at the ends. Finally, count whether enough total pairs were placed so the remaining A and B singles fit their limits.
annotation.complexity: O(|s| log |s|) per test case because alternating segment lengths are sorted.
tricks: Decompose by maximal alternating runs rather than individual characters.
Classify runs by endpoint orientation to know which pair type they naturally produce.
Greedily spend scarce AB/BA quotas on shortest exact-orientation runs before using remnants for the opposite orientation.

solution_variants.name: alternating-segment-greedy
solution_variants.summary: Scan maximal alternating segments, collect even AB-oriented and BA-oriented lengths separately, and count flexible odd segments. Sort each directed list and greedily allocate quotas, then verify the remaining single A/B counts.

tags.evidence: The main reduction is to split the string into maximal alternating segments and classify each segment.
The allocation of AB/BA quotas is solved by greedily consuming shortest matching-orientation segments before using leftovers.
Each chosen length-2 block reduces both A and B single requirements, so the algorithm balances pair counts against the single-letter quotas.
Directed alternating segments are sorted by length so the greedy allocation wastes the fewest characters.
